section.title: The Covenant in Moab
section.sectionRationale: Moses renews the covenant on the plains of Moab, reviewing all Israel has seen without yet having eyes to see, warning against the hidden root of idolatry that could poison the whole community, and closing with the sober reminder that God's secret purposes are His own, while what He has revealed is enough for obedience.
section.thesis: Standing on the plains of Moab, Moses renews the covenant with the whole nation, present and not yet born, reviewing all Israel has witnessed while confessing they still lack a heart to truly understand, warning against the hidden root of idolatry, and grounding obedience not in curiosity about God's secrets but in the sufficiency of what He has already revealed.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Moses reviews Israel's exodus and wilderness history, yet notes they still lack understanding hearts; the covenant is renewed with all Israel, present and future generations alike; a warning against the hidden root of idolatry and its self-deceived presumption; future nations imagining why such judgment fell; and the closing confession that the secret things belong to the LORD, while the revealed things belong to His people.

units.label: You have seen, yet not understood

verses.text: These are the words of the covenant that the LORD commanded Moses to make with the Israelites in the land of Moab, in addition to the covenant He had made with them at Horeb.
Moses summoned all Israel and proclaimed to them, "You have seen with your own eyes everything the LORD did in Egypt to Pharaoh, to all his officials, and to all his land.
You saw with your own eyes the great trials, and those miraculous signs and wonders.
Yet to this day the LORD has not given you a {{lev|heart}} to understand, eyes to see, or ears to hear.
For forty years I led you in the wilderness, yet your clothes and sandals did not wear out.
You ate no bread and drank no wine or strong drink, so that you might know that I am the LORD your God.
When you reached this place, Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan came out against us in battle, but we defeated them.
We took their land and gave it as an inheritance to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
So keep and follow the words of this covenant, that you may prosper in all you do.
All of you are standing today before the LORD your God, you leaders of tribes, elders, officials, and all the men of Israel,
your children and wives, and the foreigners in your camps who cut your wood and draw your water,
so that you may enter into the covenant of the LORD your God, which He is making with you today, and into His oath,
and so that He may establish you today as His people, and He may be your God as He promised you and as He swore to your fathers, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
I am making this covenant and this oath not only with you,
but also with those who are standing here with us today in the presence of the LORD our God, as well as with those who are not here today.
For you yourselves know how we lived in the land of Egypt and how we passed through the nations on the way here.
You saw the abominations and idols among them made of wood and stone, of silver and gold.

Make sure there is no man or woman, clan or tribe among you today whose heart turns away from the LORD our God to go and worship the gods of those nations. Make sure there is no {{shoresh|root}} among you that bears such poisonous and bitter fruit,

because when such a person hears the words of this oath, he invokes a blessing on himself, saying, 'I will have peace, even though I walk in the stubbornness of my own heart.' This will bring disaster on the watered land as well as the dry.

The LORD will never be willing to forgive him. Instead, His anger and jealousy will burn against that man, and every curse written in this book will fall upon him. The LORD will blot out his name from under heaven
and single him out from all the tribes of Israel for disaster, according to all the curses of the covenant written in this Book of the Law.

units.label: Why has the LORD done this?
units.note: the nations will ask, and answer

verses.text: Then the generation to come, your sons who follow you and the foreigner who comes from a distant land, will see the plagues of the land and the sicknesses the LORD has inflicted on it.

All its soil will be a burning waste of sulfur and salt, unsown and unproductive, with no plant growing on it, just like the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboiim, which the LORD overthrew in His fierce anger.
So all the nations will ask, 'Why has the LORD done such a thing to this land? Why this great outburst of anger?'
And the people will answer, 'It is because they abandoned the covenant of the LORD, the God of their fathers, which He made with them when He brought them out of the land of Egypt.
They went and served other gods, and they worshiped gods they had not known, gods that the LORD had not given to them.
Therefore the anger of the LORD burned against this land, and He brought upon it every curse written in this book.
The LORD uprooted them from their land in His anger, rage, and great wrath, and He cast them into another land, where they are today.'

units.label: The secret things belong to the LORD
units.note: and the revealed things belong to us

verses.text: The {{nistar|secret}} things belong to the LORD our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children forever, so that we may follow all the words of this law.

lev.gloss: Heart, the seat of understanding and will. 'The LORD has not given you a heart to understand' points to a deeper need than information, an inward transformation only God can give, fulfilled in the new covenant's promise of a new heart, Ezekiel 36:26.

shoresh.gloss: Root. The warning against a 'root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit' pictures apostasy as something that starts hidden and small before it spreads to devastate the whole community, echoed almost verbatim in Hebrews 12:15.

nistar.gloss: Hidden, secret. 'The secret things belong to the LORD' acknowledges real limits on what has been revealed, while what God has disclosed is fully sufficient for obedience.

basic.context: Moses gathers all Israel on the plains of Moab to renew the covenant beyond the one made at Horeb. He reviews everything they witnessed in Egypt and the wilderness, yet notes that God has still not given them a heart to truly understand. He includes every generation, present and not yet born, in this covenant, warns against any hidden root of idolatry that could poison the whole community, and imagines future nations asking why such judgment fell, before closing with the sober reminder that God's secret purposes remain His own, while His revealed word is fully sufficient for Israel's obedience.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historical covenant renewal for Israel at a specific moment in Moab, described honestly rather than idealized; Moses openly states Israel still lacks understanding, a spiritual condition Paul later explains only God Himself can reverse, Romans 11:8, quoting this very passage. The chapter's closing distinction between secret and revealed things offers wisdom for how far Christians should press into questions Scripture does not directly answer.
basic.originalLanguages: Moses says God has not given Israel a lev, heart, to understand, locating true covenant faithfulness not merely in information but in a transformed inner life. He warns of a shoresh, root, of bitterness that could poison the whole community from within. And the chapter closes distinguishing the nistar, hidden or secret things, which belong to the LORD, from what He has plainly revealed.
basic.typology: Israel's condition, having seen God's mighty acts yet lacking a heart to understand, anticipates the New Testament's diagnosis of the human condition apart from the Spirit's work, and points toward the new covenant promise of a new heart and a new spirit, Ezekiel 36:26, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.christ: Paul quotes this very chapter in Romans 11:8, 'God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that could not see and ears that could not hear,' applying Israel's condition here to explain unbelief in his own day, while insisting that in Christ a remnant, and one day 'all Israel,' will be saved, Romans 11:26.
basic.god: God gives real revelation and real evidence, the plagues, the wilderness provision, the victories, yet also acknowledges that seeing is not the same as understanding; only He can give the heart that truly receives what He has shown, and He alone holds the secret things that remain beyond human curiosity.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 11:8 quotes this chapter's language of spiritual blindness. Ezekiel 36:26 promises the new heart this chapter shows Israel still lacks. 2 Corinthians 3:14-16 says a veil remains over hearts reading the old covenant until they turn to the Lord. Hebrews 12:15 echoes the warning against a 'root of bitterness' springing up to defile many.
basic.oneStory: Israel's covenant renewal here exposes a problem no law, however good, can fix: a heart still unable to understand. That very problem drives the story toward the new covenant, where God Himself promises to give His people a new heart, fulfilled fully in Christ and the Spirit He gives.

context.literary: This chapter opens the covenant renewal narrative proper, following the curse ceremony of chapters 27-28, and leads directly into chapter 30's call to choose life.
context.historical: Covenant renewal ceremonies, restating and re-ratifying a treaty relationship for a new generation or new circumstance, were a known ancient Near Eastern practice; Moses adapts this form for Israel on the plains of Moab, forty years after the original Horeb covenant.
context.canonical: Paul's direct citation of this chapter in Romans 11 to explain Israel's ongoing unbelief in his own day shows this passage functioning as a key text for New Testament theology of Israel, hardening, and grace.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To renew Israel's covenant with the LORD across every generation, honestly naming Israel's continuing spiritual blindness even after witnessing God's mighty acts, warning against hidden apostasy, and grounding obedience in what God has revealed rather than speculation about what He has not.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific historical covenant renewal ceremony belongs to Israel's unique moment on the plains of Moab; its enduring principles, honest acknowledgment of spiritual blindness apart from God's own gracious work, vigilance against hidden roots of apostasy, and contentment with what God has revealed rather than demanding what He has not, carry forward as abiding wisdom.

debates.question: What does verse 29's distinction between 'secret things' and 'revealed things' mean for how Christians should handle difficult or unanswered theological questions?

views.label: A call to humble contentment with Scripture's actual limits
views.case: This verse teaches that some matters, the timing of events, the ultimate reasons behind God's specific choices, remain God's own prerogative, and pressing speculative curiosity beyond what is revealed distracts from the practical obedience Scripture actually calls for.

views.label: A reminder that revelation itself, not human reasoning, is the boundary of certainty
views.case: The verse also implies real confidence: what God has revealed is fully 'ours and our children's forever,' sufficient and trustworthy for obedience, so believers need not treat every doctrine as uncertain simply because some mysteries remain.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Scripture gives real, sufficient revelation for obedience while some matters remain properly beyond human access, calling for both confidence in what is revealed and humility about what is not.

study.originalLanguages: Moses says God has not given Israel a lev, heart, verse 4, to understand. He warns of a shoresh, root, verse 18, of bitterness. And the chapter closes distinguishing the nistar, secret, verse 29, things belonging to the LORD from what He has plainly revealed.

typology.type: Israel's blindness despite witnessing God's mighty acts
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's diagnosis of unbelief and its promise of a new heart given by the Spirit, Ezekiel 36:26, Romans 11:8.

typology.type: The covenant renewed for every generation, present and future
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant in Christ's blood, likewise made for 'you and for many,' reaching every generation who believes, Luke 22:20.

study.christ: Paul quotes this very chapter in Romans 11:8, 'God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that could not see and ears that could not hear,' applying Israel's condition here to explain unbelief in his own day, while insisting that in Christ a remnant, and one day 'all Israel,' will be saved, Romans 11:26.
study.god: God gives real revelation and real evidence, the plagues, the wilderness provision, the victories, yet also acknowledges that seeing is not the same as understanding; only He can give the heart that truly receives what He has shown, and He alone holds the secret things that remain beyond human curiosity.

crossReferences.note: Quotes this chapter's language of spiritual blindness to explain Israel's unbelief.
Promises the new heart this chapter shows Israel still lacks.
Says a veil remains over hearts reading the old covenant until they turn to the Lord.
Echoes the warning against a 'root of bitterness' springing up to defile many.

study.oneStory: Israel's covenant renewal here exposes a problem no law, however good, can fix: a heart still unable to understand. That very problem drives the story toward the new covenant, where God Himself promises to give His people a new heart, fulfilled fully in Christ and the Spirit He gives.

sermon.title: An Impossible Covenant, And How It Becomes a Permanent Joy
sermon.why: Piper works directly through Deuteronomy 29, including verses 4, 13, 18-21, and 29's 'the secret things belong to the Lord,' to show Israel's spiritual blindness apart from God's grace and the sufficiency of what He has revealed, matching this study's passage and focus.

quiz.q: According to verse 4, what does Moses say Israel still lacks, despite witnessing God's great acts?

options.text: A heart to understand, eyes to see, or ears to hear.
options.note: Verse 4 states this explicitly.

options.text: Enough food and water in the wilderness.
options.note: Verses 5-6 describe God's provision, not lack, during the wilderness years.

options.text: Military strength to defeat their enemies.
options.note: The immediate context describes their victories over Sihon and Og, not military lack.

options.text: Nothing; Israel fully understood everything.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly says the opposite.

quiz.q: Who is included in the covenant being renewed in this chapter, according to verses 14-15?

options.text: Both those standing there that day and those not yet present, future generations.
options.note: Verses 14-15 state this explicitly.

options.text: Only the adult men present that day.
options.note: Verses 14-15 explicitly extend the covenant beyond those physically present.

options.text: Only the priests and Levites.
options.note: Verses 10-11 describe a much broader inclusion, not limited to priests.

options.text: No mention is made of who the covenant applies to.
options.note: Verses 14-15 give explicit inclusion.

quiz.q: What image does Moses use in verse 18 to warn against hidden idolatry?

options.text: A root that bears poisonous and bitter fruit.
options.note: Verse 18 uses this exact image.

options.text: A storm that destroys crops instantly.
options.note: The image given is a hidden, growing root, not a sudden storm.

options.text: A wild animal roaming the camp.
options.note: The image is botanical, a root, not an animal.

options.text: No image is used.
options.note: Verse 18 gives an explicit image.

quiz.q: According to verses 24-28, how will future nations explain Israel's coming judgment?

options.text: That Israel abandoned the covenant of the LORD and served other gods.
options.note: Verses 25-26 state this explicit explanation the nations will give.

options.text: That Israel simply had bad luck.
options.note: The text gives a specific covenantal explanation, not chance.

options.text: That the surrounding nations were simply too powerful.
options.note: The stated reason is Israel's own covenant unfaithfulness, not enemy strength.

options.text: No explanation is offered.
options.note: Verses 24-28 give a detailed, explicit explanation.

quiz.q: What does verse 29 say belongs to the LORD, as opposed to what belongs to Israel?

options.text: The secret things belong to the LORD, while the things revealed belong to Israel and their children forever.
options.note: Verse 29 states this exact distinction.

options.text: Everything belongs to the LORD, and nothing has been revealed to Israel.
options.note: Verse 29 explicitly says revealed things belong to Israel and their children.

options.text: Nothing belongs to the LORD; all things are revealed.
options.note: The verse explicitly reserves some things as secret, belonging to the LORD.

options.text: No such distinction is made.
options.note: Verse 29 makes this distinction explicit.

questions: Israel had seen God's mighty acts firsthand yet still lacked a heart to truly understand. Where do you find yourself intellectually aware of what God has done but not truly changed by it in your heart?

This covenant was made not only with those present but with generations not yet born. How does knowing you are included in God's ongoing covenant purposes, long planned before you existed, shape your sense of belonging to Him?

Moses warned against a hidden root of idolatry that could poison the whole community. What small compromise in your own heart might be quietly growing into something that could damage those around you?

The surrounding nations would one day ask why judgment fell on Israel, and the answer was covenant unfaithfulness. Is there an area of your life where continued unfaithfulness is heading toward consequences you are choosing not to see?
Moses says the secret things belong to the LORD, but what is revealed belongs to us. Where are you more preoccupied with unanswerable questions than with obeying what God has already made plain?

translationNotes.note: This verse is quoted by Paul in Romans 11:8 to explain a form of spiritual hardening that only God's own grace can reverse, tying this Old Testament diagnosis directly to New Testament teaching on unbelief and grace.
'A root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit' is echoed almost verbatim in Hebrews 12:15, warning New Testament believers against the same danger of hidden, spreading apostasy within a community.
'The secret things belong to the LORD' is a foundational text for the doctrine that Scripture, while not answering every possible question, reveals everything necessary for faith and obedience.
